title: The Product Rule

options.feedback: You might have subtracted the exponents (3 − (−10)). Remember that the Product Law requires you to add exponents when multiplying the same bases.
Correct! 🎉 You nailed it — you added the exponents perfectly.
You multiplied the exponents (3 × −10). The Product Law says to add them: 3 + (−10).
You treated the exponents like coefficients and multiplied them by the base. Exponents tell you how many times to multiply the base by itself.

hint: Use the Product Law: a^m × a^n = a^(m+n).

title: Negative Neighbors

options.feedback: Don't multiply the exponents! Add them together, even if they are both negative.
Great job! 🔥 You correctly added the negative exponents.
It looks like you multiplied the base by the sum of the exponents (−2 times −7). Exponents are not multipliers; they represent repeated multiplication.
Check your addition of negative numbers. −3 + (−4) is not −1.

hint: When bases are identical, add the negative exponents.

title: Mixed Signs

options.feedback: You multiplied −2 × 4. The Product Law states we must add them: −2 + 4.
Awesome! 💯 You handled positive and negative exponents perfectly.
A negative exponent like 5^−2 does not make the result negative; it creates a fraction.
You added the absolute values (2 + 4) but ignored the negative sign on the first exponent.

hint: Add the exponents 2 and -4 using the product rule.

options.feedback: Boom! 💥 That was smooth — you added all exponents correctly.
You multiplied all three exponents (2 × −5 × 6). You should add them instead.
It looks like you multiplied the base 3 by the final exponent 3. Exponents indicate the number of times the base is used as a factor.
You added all numbers (2 + 5 + 6) but forgot that one exponent was negative.

title: The Zero Power

options.feedback: Any non-zero number raised to the power of 0 is always 1, not 0.
Correct! 🎉 Classic rule — any non-zero number to power 0 is 1.
You treated the 0 exponent as if it had no effect on the base. Remember, a^0 = 1.
It seems you ignored the 5 and the exponent. Solve the expression inside the parentheses first, then apply the exponent.

hint: Any non-zero base raised to the power of zero equals one.
full_solution: (100 + 5)^0 = 105^0. Since any non-zero number to the power of 0 is 1, the answer is 1.

title: Division Decisions

options.feedback: You added the exponents (5 +(- 8)). In division, you must subtract the second exponent from the first.
Excellent! 🔥 You handled the double negative perfectly while subtracting exponents.
You multiplied the base −4 by the exponent −3. Exponents represent repeated multiplication.
You subtracted -8 − 5 instead of 5 − (-8). Order matters in the Quotient Law!

hint: Use the division rule: a^m ÷ a^n = a^(m−n).

options.feedback: You subtracted 5 − 6. But the exponent is −6, so you must subtract a negative: 5 − (−6).
Great job! 🎯 You correctly converted subtraction of a negative into addition.
You multiplied the exponents (5 × −6). Use the subtraction rule for division.
It seems there was a subtraction error. Remember that subtracting a negative is the same as adding a positive.

title: Power of a Power

options.feedback: You added the exponents (−3 + 2). For a "power of a power," you must multiply them.
Awesome! 💯 You applied the power rule perfectly.
A negative exponent (2^−6) means 1/2^6, not a negative integer.
It looks like there was a calculation error. Multiply −3 by 2.

hint: Multiply the exponents together using the rule (a^m)^n = a^(mn).

title: Different Bases

options.feedback: You added the exponents, but the bases are different (a and b). You can only add exponents if the bases are the same.
Nice work! 😎 You correctly combined the bases while keeping the exponent.
Negative exponents don't change the sign of the base; they represent a reciprocal.
You multiplied the exponents and changed the sign. The rule is to keep the same exponent when bases are multiplied.

hint: Since the exponents are the same, multiply the bases and keep the exponent.
full_solution: Using the law a^m × b^m = (ab)^m, we get a^−3 × b^−3 = (ab)^−3

title: Solving for m
question_text: Find the value of m such that (−3)^(m+1) × (−3)^5 = (−3)^7.

options.feedback: Brilliant! 🧠 You solved the exponent equation perfectly.
You might have assumed m + 5 = 7, forgetting the +1 in the first exponent.
It seems you are trying to divide by the base. Focus only on the exponents since the bases are already identical on both sides.
Check your algebraic steps. (m + 1) + 5 = 7 leads to m + 6 = 7.

hint: Add the exponents on the left side and set them equal to the exponent on the right.
full_solution: (−3)^(m+1) × (−3)^5 = (−3)^(m+6). So Since bases are the same, m + 6 = 7 → m = 1.

title: Complex Combination

options.feedback: It looks like you solved the bracket but missed the final multiplication step with 2^−5.
Impressive! 💯 You handled multiple exponent rules step-by-step flawlessly.
Check the sign after division (5 − 8 = −3) and power ((−3)×5 = −15).
You may have added exponents where multiplication was required.

hint: Work step-by-step: first division (subtract), then power of power (multiply), then product (add).

options.feedback: Although this problem has a negative base, the exponent is even ($4$). A negative number raised to an even power becomes positive
Excellent! 🎉 You correctly combined the bases and handled the sign perfectly.
You multiplied the bases but ignored the division by 3 inside the second base
Combined incorrectly.
